ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Endocrine System Overview for Nurses
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Endocrine System Overview for Nurses | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Clinical review: Endocrine System Overview for Nurses — Endocrine. Board-style clinical judgment: priorities, red flags, and first-line nursing actions.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Endocrine System Overview for Nurses** (Endocrine) links glucose regulation, thyroid and adrenal axes, pituitary disorders, and fluid–electrolyte balance to nursing judgment: recognize **hypoglycemia and hyperglycemic emergencies**, **thyroid storm and myxedema coma**, **adrenal crisis**, **severe sodium disorders** from SIADH/DI, **calcium emergencies**, and **medication errors** involving insulin, steroids, and thyroid drugs—and escalate when perfusion, airway, or mentation is threatened.

Canadian items may use **SI labs** (mmol/L for glucose where shown) and provincial diabetes program wording; prioritization logic matches NCLEX-RN.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly framing. Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **glucose trends, ketones, insulin timing, potassium and sodium trends, vitals, neuro status, and weight/I&O** to identify endocrine emergencies and complications.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, endocrinology and pharmacy plans, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **symptomatic hypoglycemia**, **DKA/HHS**, **thyroid storm**, **myxedema coma**, **addisonian crisis**, **severe hypernatremia or hyponatremia**, or **pheochromocytoma hypertensive crisis**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Endocrine System Overview for Nurses** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Items tie **hypothalamus–pituitary–target gland** axes, **feedback inhibition**, and **clinical syndromes** (too much vs too little hormone)—prioritize **recognizing the axis in trouble** before memorizing isolated drug names.

Eliminate answers that **skip glucose or neuro checks**, **delay potassium safety** before insulin in DKA, **give free water** inappropriately in SIADH, or **delegate** unstable assessment. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Axes**: HPA, HPT, HPG, RAAS overlap themes; glucose–insulin–glucagon as fast loop.
- **Assessment**: vitals pattern, skin/hair, glucose trends, BP, weight, neuro status.
- **Safety**: never treat numbers without symptoms + orders—especially Na and glucose.
- **Integration**: infection, stress, surgery shift cortisol and glucose needs in vignettes.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hypoglycemia with altered LOC or seizure**: **glucose rescue** per protocol (oral if awake/safe swallow; **IV dextrose** or **glucagon** when unconscious)—never insulin first.
- **DKA/HHS**: **volume + insulin pathway** with **K⁺ checked before and during insulin**; watch for **cerebral edema** themes in pediatric DKA when stem hints.
- **Thyroid storm**: **fever + tachycardia + neuro** cluster—cooling, **beta-blockade** per orders, **antithyroid** therapy; **myxedema coma**: **hypothermia + hypoventilation**—airway, gentle rewarming, **thyroid replacement** per ICU protocol.
- **Adrenal crisis**: **hypotension + hyponatremia + hyperkalemia + hypoglycemia**—stress-dose steroid themes with fluids per orders; never abrupt steroid cessation after chronic use.
- **SIADH**: **fluid restriction** and **slow sodium correction**; **central DI**: **replace water deficit** and **desmopressin** per orders—do not confuse the two fluid strategies.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **sick-day rules**, **insulin storage and rotation**, **hypoglycemia recognition and glucagon use**, **steroid taper adherence**, **thyroid drug timing**, and **when to seek emergency care**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when glucose, sodium, potassium, airway, or perfusion is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client scenario related to **mapping hormones to organs before a stem clusters polyuria, goiter, and glucose extremes** brings endocrine risks to the foreground. You pair **focused assessment** (glucose, neuro status, vitals, weight, I&O, breath pattern, skin temperature) with **labs** when provided, follow **insulin and steroid orders precisely**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually **glucose rescue**, **K⁺ safety check**, **airway support**, **volume resuscitation per order**, **hold harmful fluids/meds**, or **continuous monitoring**—rather than routine comfort measures alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **peaked T waves with rising K during DKA treatment**, **temperature >39 with tachyarrhythmia in thyrotoxicosis**, or **sodium correcting too fast with neuro change**, prioritize **protocol-driven reversal and escalation**.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **glucose + ketones + K⁺ + Na⁺ + vitals + mentation** (and **insulin pump/CGM** checks when relevant) before routine tasks when endocrine risk is rising.
- Use the Endocrine topic hub (**`endocrine`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.
- Link review: [DKA vs HHS](LESSON:dka-vs-hhs-priorities-hy) · [SIADH vs DI basics](LESSON:siadh-vs-di-basics) · [Addisonian crisis](LESSON:addisonian-crisis) · [thyroid storm & myxedema](LESSON:thyroid-storm-myxedema-clues) · [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons)

**Takeaway drill:** Name the **life threat**, the **two objective findings** you recheck first, and the **single escalation** that matches policy—then pick the answer that matches that sequence.

- **SIADH**: **fluid restriction** and **slow sodium correction**; **central DI**: **replace water deficit** and **desmopressin** per orders—do not confuse the two fluid strategies.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Insulin Administration and Timing** (Endocrine) links glucose regulation, thyroid and adrenal axes, pituitary disorders, and fluid–electrolyte balance to nursing judgment: recognize **hypoglycemia and hyperglycemic emergencies**, **thyroid storm and myxedema coma**, **adrenal crisis**, **severe sodium disorders** from SIADH/DI, **calcium emergencies**, and **medication errors** involving insulin, steroids, and thyroid drugs—and escalate when perfusion, airway, or mentation is threatened.

Canadian items may use **SI labs** (mmol/L for glucose where shown) and provincial diabetes program wording; prioritization logic matches NCLEX-RN.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly framing. Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **glucose trends, ketones, insulin timing, potassium and sodium trends, vitals, neuro status, and weight/I&O** to identify endocrine emergencies and complications.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, endocrinology and pharmacy plans, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **symptomatic hypoglycemia**, **DKA/HHS**, **thyroid storm**, **myxedema coma**, **addisonian crisis**, **severe hypernatremia or hyponatremia**, or **pheochromocytoma hypertensive crisis**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Insulin Administration and Timing** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Match **insulin type to action profile**, **meal timing**, **site rotation**, and **never mix** unless order and technique correct—boards test **aspiration** with NPH/regular mixing errors and **stacking** rapid doses.

Eliminate answers that **skip glucose or neuro checks**, **delay potassium safety** before insulin in DKA, **give free water** inappropriately in SIADH, or **delegate** unstable assessment. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Rapid**: with meals or per correction scale; know onset/peak themes for hypoglycemia watch windows.
- **Basal**: time consistency; explain missed dose protocol per order set.
- **U-500 / concentrated**: double-check dosing; separate pen/syringe teaching.
- **Inpatient**: hold if NPO per protocol—verify rather than guess.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hypoglycemia with altered LOC or seizure**: **glucose rescue** per protocol (oral if awake/safe swallow; **IV dextrose** or **glucagon** when unconscious)—never insulin first.
- **DKA/HHS**: **volume + insulin pathway** with **K⁺ checked before and during insulin**; watch for **cerebral edema** themes in pediatric DKA when stem hints.
- **Thyroid storm**: **fever + tachycardia + neuro** cluster—cooling, **beta-blockade** per orders, **antithyroid** therapy; **myxedema coma**: **hypothermia + hypoventilation**—airway, gentle rewarming, **thyroid replacement** per ICU protocol.
- **Adrenal crisis**: **hypotension + hyponatremia + hyperkalemia + hypoglycemia**—stress-dose steroid themes with fluids per orders; never abrupt steroid cessation after chronic use.
- **SIADH**: **fluid restriction** and **slow sodium correction**; **central DI**: **replace water deficit** and **desmopressin** per orders—do not confuse the two fluid strategies.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **sick-day rules**, **insulin storage and rotation**, **hypoglycemia recognition and glucagon use**, **steroid taper adherence**, **thyroid drug timing**, and **when to seek emergency care**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when glucose, sodium, potassium, airway, or perfusion is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

**Thiazide and thiazide-like diuretics (high-yield monitoring).** Used for **hypertension**, **edema**, and sometimes **nephrogenic DI** paradox. sections.heading: Core concept

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Adrenal Insufficiency (Addison’s Disease)** (Endocrine) links glucose regulation, thyroid and adrenal axes, pituitary disorders, and fluid–electrolyte balance to nursing judgment: recognize **hypoglycemia and hyperglycemic emergencies**, **thyroid storm and myxedema coma**, **adrenal crisis**, **severe sodium disorders** from SIADH/DI, **calcium emergencies**, and **medication errors** involving insulin, steroids, and thyroid drugs—and escalate when perfusion, airway, or mentation is threatened.

**Primary vs secondary (quick exam frame).** **Primary** adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease) destroys the **adrenal cortex** → low **cortisol** and usually **aldosterone** deficiency → **hyponatremia**, **hyperkalemia**, **hypotension**, **hypoglycemia**, **hyperpigmentation** (ACTH-driven melanocyte stimulation). **Secondary** pituitary ACTH failure spares mineralocorticoid early—electrolyte patterns may differ. Teaching emphasizes **never abrupt steroid withdrawal**, **stress-dose** plans per written endocrinology guidance, **Medic-Alert** jewelry, and **extra glucocorticoid** during febrile illness per protocol—not ad-lib dose doubling.

Differentiate **chronic primary AI** education from **Addisonian crisis**—still test **stress dosing** plans, **Medic-Alert**, **never abrupt steroid stop**, and **electrolyte** surveillance.

Eliminate answers that **skip glucose or neuro checks**, **delay potassium safety** before insulin in DKA, **give free water** inappropriately in SIADH, or **delegate** unstable assessment. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept

sections.heading: Core concept

**Cushing Syndrome vs Cushing Disease** (Endocrine) links glucose regulation, thyroid and adrenal axes, pituitary disorders, and fluid–electrolyte balance to nursing judgment: recognize **hypoglycemia and hyperglycemic emergencies**, **thyroid storm and myxedema coma**, **adrenal crisis**, **severe sodium disorders** from SIADH/DI, **calcium emergencies**, and **medication errors** involving insulin, steroids, and thyroid drugs—and escalate when perfusion, airway, or mentation is threatened.

US NCLEX-RN items often test **DKA vs HHS**, **insulin and potassium safety**, **thyroid storm vs myxedema**, **adrenal crisis**, and **SIADH vs DI** fluid decisions.

**Pathway context (RN, United States).** Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **glucose trends, ketones, insulin timing, potassium and sodium trends, vitals, neuro status, and weight/I&O** to identify endocrine emergencies and complications.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, endocrinology and pharmacy plans, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **symptomatic hypoglycemia**, **DKA/HHS**, **thyroid storm**, **myxedema coma**, **addisonian crisis**, **severe hypernatremia or hyponatremia**, or **pheochromocytoma hypertensive crisis**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Cushing Syndrome vs Cushing Disease** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

**Syndrome** = cortisol excess from any cause; **Disease** = **pituitary ACTH** excess—items test **medication history**, **dexamethasone suppression** logic when labs shown, and **nursing** focus on **infection, glucose, BP, mood, bone** risks.

Eliminate answers that **skip glucose or neuro checks**, **delay potassium safety** before insulin in DKA, **give free water** inappropriately in SIADH, or **delegate** unstable assessment. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Iatrogenic**: taper plans; never abrupt stop after prolonged use.
- **Assessment**: striae, proximal weakness, buffalo hump, hyperglycemia.
- **Complications**: DVT, wound infection, psychosis—safety first.
- **Post-op**: pituitary surgery fluid/diabetes insipidus surveillance themes when stem follows surgery.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hypoglycemia with altered LOC or seizure**: **glucose rescue** per protocol (oral if awake/safe swallow; **IV dextrose** or **glucagon** when unconscious)—never insulin first.
- **DKA/HHS**: **volume + insulin pathway** with **K⁺ checked before and during insulin**; watch for **cerebral edema** themes in pediatric DKA when stem hints.
- **Thyroid storm**: **fever + tachycardia + neuro** cluster—cooling, **beta-blockade** per orders, **antithyroid** therapy; **myxedema coma**: **hypothermia + hypoventilation**—airway, gentle rewarming, **thyroid replacement** per ICU protocol.
- **Adrenal crisis**: **hypotension + hyponatremia + hyperkalemia + hypoglycemia**—stress-dose steroid themes with fluids per orders; never abrupt steroid cessation after chronic use.
- **SIADH**: **fluid restriction** and **slow sodium correction**; **central DI**: **replace water deficit** and **desmopressin** per orders—do not confuse the two fluid strategies.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **sick-day rules**, **insulin storage and rotation**, **hypoglycemia recognition and glucagon use**, **steroid taper adherence**, **thyroid drug timing**, and **when to seek emergency care**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when glucose, sodium, potassium, airway, or perfusion is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client scenario related to **exogenous steroid-induced cushingoid appearance versus ACTH-secreting pituitary adenoma workup** brings endocrine risks to the foreground. You pair **focused assessment** (glucose, neuro status, vitals, weight, I&O, breath pattern, skin temperature) with **labs** when provided, follow **insulin and steroid orders precisely**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually **glucose rescue**, **K⁺ safety check**, **airway support**, **volume resuscitation per order**, **hold harmful fluids/meds**, or **continuous monitoring**—rather than routine comfort measures alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **peaked T waves with rising K during DKA treatment**, **temperature >39 with tachyarrhythmia in thyrotoxicosis**, or **sodium correcting too fast with neuro change**, prioritize **protocol-driven reversal and escalation**.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **glucose + ketones + K⁺ + Na⁺ + vitals + mentation** (and **insulin pump/CGM** checks when relevant) before routine tasks when endocrine risk is rising.

**Growth Hormone Disorders (Acromegaly, Dwarfism)** (Endocrine) links glucose regulation, thyroid and adrenal axes, pituitary disorders, and fluid–electrolyte balance to nursing judgment: recognize **hypoglycemia and hyperglycemic emergencies**, **thyroid storm and myxedema coma**, **adrenal crisis**, **severe sodium disorders** from SIADH/DI, **calcium emergencies**, and **medication errors** involving insulin, steroids, and thyroid drugs—and escalate when perfusion, airway, or mentation is threatened.

US NCLEX-RN items often test **DKA vs HHS**, **insulin and potassium safety**, **thyroid storm vs myxedema**, **adrenal crisis**, and **SIADH vs DI** fluid decisions.

**Pathway context (RN, United States).** Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **glucose trends, ketones, insulin timing, potassium and sodium trends, vitals, neuro status, and weight/I&O** to identify endocrine emergencies and complications.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, endocrinology and pharmacy plans, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **symptomatic hypoglycemia**, **DKA/HHS**, **thyroid storm**, **myxedema coma**, **addisonian crisis**, **severe hypernatremia or hyponatremia**, or **pheochromocytoma hypertensive crisis**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Growth Hormone Disorders (Acromegaly, Dwarfism)** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

**Acromegaly** complications: **OSA**, **HTN**, **DM**, **joint pain**, **carpal tunnel**—teach **surgery/radiation/medical therapy** adherence; **dwarfism** items focus **growth hormone replacement** monitoring for **intracranial hypertension** signs in pediatric stems when listed.

Eliminate answers that **skip glucose or neuro checks**, **delay potassium safety** before insulin in DKA, **give free water** inappropriately in SIADH, or **delegate** unstable assessment. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Acromegaly**: glucose and BP control; visual field checks if mass.
- **GH deficiency**: childhood growth velocity tracking; injection site rotation.
- **Safety**: pituitary mass → same neuro vigilance as other pituitary lesions.
- **Support**: body image and adherence counseling when vignette includes psychosocial tone.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hypoglycemia with altered LOC or seizure**: **glucose rescue** per protocol (oral if awake/safe swallow; **IV dextrose** or **glucagon** when unconscious)—never insulin first.
- **DKA/HHS**: **volume + insulin pathway** with **K⁺ checked before and during insulin**; watch for **cerebral edema** themes in pediatric DKA when stem hints.
- **Thyroid storm**: **fever + tachycardia + neuro** cluster—cooling, **beta-blockade** per orders, **antithyroid** therapy; **myxedema coma**: **hypothermia + hypoventilation**—airway, gentle rewarming, **thyroid replacement** per ICU protocol.
- **Adrenal crisis**: **hypotension + hyponatremia + hyperkalemia + hypoglycemia**—stress-dose steroid themes with fluids per orders; never abrupt steroid cessation after chronic use.
- **SIADH**: **fluid restriction** and **slow sodium correction**; **central DI**: **replace water deficit** and **desmopressin** per orders—do not confuse the two fluid strategies.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **sick-day rules**, **insulin storage and rotation**, **hypoglycemia recognition and glucagon use**, **steroid taper adherence**, **thyroid drug timing**, and **when to seek emergency care**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when glucose, sodium, potassium, airway, or perfusion is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client scenario related to **enlarging ring size and sleep apnea in adult with frontal bossing photo cue** brings endocrine risks to the foreground. You pair **focused assessment** (glucose, neuro status, vitals, weight, I&O, breath pattern, skin temperature) with **labs** when provided, follow **insulin and steroid orders precisely**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually **glucose rescue**, **K⁺ safety check**, **airway support**, **volume resuscitation per order**, **hold harmful fluids/meds**, or **continuous monitoring**—rather than routine comfort measures alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **peaked T waves with rising K during DKA treatment**, **temperature >39 with tachyarrhythmia in thyrotoxicosis**, or **sodium correcting too fast with neuro change**, prioritize **protocol-driven reversal and escalation**.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:

**What Do You Do First? (Endocrine Prioritization)** (Endocrine) links glucose regulation, thyroid and adrenal axes, pituitary disorders, and fluid–electrolyte balance to nursing judgment: recognize **hypoglycemia and hyperglycemic emergencies**, **thyroid storm and myxedema coma**, **adrenal crisis**, **severe sodium disorders** from SIADH/DI, **calcium emergencies**, and **medication errors** involving insulin, steroids, and thyroid drugs—and escalate when perfusion, airway, or mentation is threatened.

US NCLEX-RN items often test **DKA vs HHS**, **insulin and potassium safety**, **thyroid storm vs myxedema**, **adrenal crisis**, and **SIADH vs DI** fluid decisions.

**Pathway context (RN, United States).** Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **glucose trends, ketones, insulin timing, potassium and sodium trends, vitals, neuro status, and weight/I&O** to identify endocrine emergencies and complications.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, endocrinology and pharmacy plans, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **symptomatic hypoglycemia**, **DKA/HHS**, **thyroid storm**, **myxedema coma**, **addisonian crisis**, **severe hypernatremia or hyponatremia**, or **pheochromocytoma hypertensive crisis**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **What Do You Do First? (Endocrine Prioritization)** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

**Unconscious hypoglycemia** → **airway**, **dextrose IV if access**, **glucagon** if no IV per BLS/ALS—**fingerstick** if rapid and does not delay treatment in many items; **DKA** first actions often **IV access + labs + fluid bolus** per order set before insulin when K unsafe.

Eliminate answers that **skip glucose or neuro checks**, **delay potassium safety** before insulin in DKA, **give free water** inappropriately in SIADH, or **delegate** unstable assessment. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Thyroid storm**: cooling, beta-blocker per order, antithyroid—sequence per item.
- **Myxedema coma**: airway, gentle rewarming, thyroid hormone—avoid rapid rewarming alone.
- **Adrenal crisis**: fluids + steroids per orders—recognition first.
- **Never**: insulin for unconscious hypoglycemia before glucose rescue.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hypoglycemia with altered LOC or seizure**: **glucose rescue** per protocol (oral if awake/safe swallow; **IV dextrose** or **glucagon** when unconscious)—never insulin first.
- **DKA/HHS**: **volume + insulin pathway** with **K⁺ checked before and during insulin**; watch for **cerebral edema** themes in pediatric DKA when stem hints.
- **Thyroid storm**: **fever + tachycardia + neuro** cluster—cooling, **beta-blockade** per orders, **antithyroid** therapy; **myxedema coma**: **hypothermia + hypoventilation**—airway, gentle rewarming, **thyroid replacement** per ICU protocol.
- **Adrenal crisis**: **hypotension + hyponatremia + hyperkalemia + hypoglycemia**—stress-dose steroid themes with fluids per orders; never abrupt steroid cessation after chronic use.
- **SIADH**: **fluid restriction** and **slow sodium correction**; **central DI**: **replace water deficit** and **desmopressin** per orders—do not confuse the two fluid strategies.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **sick-day rules**, **insulin storage and rotation**, **hypoglycemia recognition and glucagon use**, **steroid taper adherence**, **thyroid drug timing**, and **when to seek emergency care**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when glucose, sodium, potassium, airway, or perfusion is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client scenario related to **unconscious known diabetic—first action among glucagon, IV dextrose, and fingerstick** brings endocrine risks to the foreground. You pair **focused assessment** (glucose, neuro status, vitals, weight, I&O, breath pattern, skin temperature) with **labs** when provided, follow **insulin and steroid orders precisely**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually **glucose rescue**, **K⁺ safety check**, **airway support**, **volume resuscitation per order**, **hold harmful fluids/meds**, or **continuous monitoring**—rather than routine comfort measures alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **peaked T waves with rising K during DKA treatment**, **temperature >39 with tachyarrhythmia in thyrotoxicosis**, or **sodium correcting too fast with neuro change**, prioritize **protocol-driven reversal and escalation**.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **glucose + ketones + K⁺ + Na⁺ + vitals + mentation** (and **insulin pump/CGM** checks when relevant) before routine tasks when endocrine risk is rising.
- Use the Endocrine topic hub (**`endocrine`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.
- Link review: [DKA vs HHS](LESSON:dka-vs-hhs-priorities-hy) · [SIADH vs DI basics](LESSON:siadh-vs-di-basics) · [Addisonian crisis](LESSON:addisonian-crisis) · [thyroid storm & myxedema](LESSON:thyroid-storm-myxedema-clues) · [pathway lesson hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons)

**Takeaway drill:** Name the **life threat**, the **two objective findings** you recheck first, and the **single escalation** that matches policy—then pick the answer that matches that sequence.
